title: The tags that say what a thing is
checkLabels.t11: The links are inside the nav
checkLabels.t12: The title is in the top part
checkLabels.t13: The listing is in the main
checkLabels.t15: The copyright is at the bottom
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted everything, the page included.
why.untouched: This is exactly the code I gave you. Nothing has been changed.
hint2.untouched: Do not delete the content. Swap the wrappers — the groups top, menu, middle, listing and bottom all have real tags.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
hint2.not-closed:
If you swap a
for a
, swap the
for a too. A forgotten closing tag is the most common mistake here.
why.lost: You deleted the content. The task was to swap the wrappers, not remove the page. Bring back the title, the listing and the price.
why.no-header: The top part is still a group labelled top. Top is a word you invented. There is a tag the browser understands.
hint2.no-header: Look at the first <div> in the body. It holds the logo and the menu — that is the top of the page.
hint4.no-header: Swap that <div> for a <header>. Do not touch what is inside.
why.no-nav: The menu is still a group. There is a tag for navigation — you have known it since lesson 35.
hint2.no-nav: The group labelled menu holds a list of links. There is a tag for exactly that.
hint4.no-nav: Swap <div class="menu"> for <nav>. The list inside stays as it is.
why.no-main: There is no main content. If everything on the page is equally important, nothing is. <main> says: this is the point, the rest is setting.
hint2.no-main: The group labelled middle is the point of this page. The logo and the bottom line repeat everywhere — they are not.
why.no-article: The listing is a group. But it is a standalone thing — lift it out of the page and it still makes sense. That is exactly what <article> means.
hint2.no-article: The group labelled listing holds the heading and price of one bike. Put it on another page — it still makes sense.
hint3.no-article: The test: does a comment under an article make sense on its own? Yes — <article>. Does an About us section? No — <section>.
why.no-footer: The bottom part is still a group. <footer> says: the content ends here.
hint2.no-footer: The last <div> in the body holds the copyright. There is a tag for the bottom part.
why.nav-empty: The navigation is there, but it is empty. The links have been left outside it — the reader will say navigation, 0 links.
hint2.nav-empty: Check where you close the </nav>. Close it before the list and the list stays outside.
hint3.nav-empty:
Here is the mistake:
  • Home
. The navigation is an empty box and the links sit next to it.
hint4.nav-empty:
opens before the
    and closes after the
. Both links must be between them.
why.h1-outside: The site title has been left outside the top part. The logo and the menu belong together — they are one thing.
hint2.h1-outside: Open the <header> above the <h1>, not below it.
why.article-outside: The listing is outside the main content. It IS the point of this page — its place is inside <main>.
hint2.article-outside: Check where you close the </main>. Close it straight away and the listing stays outside.
hint3.article-outside: Nesting is a box in a box: <main><article>...</article></main>. Not side by side.
why.many-main: There is more than one <main>. It is special: exactly one per page. Two main contents means zero main contents.
hint2.many-main: The bottom part is not main content. Neither is the top. They repeat on every page of the site.
why.footer-empty: The bottom part is there, but the copyright has been left outside it.
hint2.footer-empty: <footer> opens before the paragraph with Copyright 2026 and closes after it.
why.still-div: There is still a group left. Each of the five wrappers in this lesson has a real tag of its own — nothing here needs a <div>.
hint2.still-div: Search the editor with Ctrl+F for the word div. Every hit is a wrapper that has a real name.
hint4.still-div: The five are: top, menu, middle, listing, bottom. Five groups, five tags.
blocks.0.text: The code on the right is better than the last one — it has real headings, a real list. But the wrappers are still groups: top, middle, bottom.
blocks.1.text: Those words you invented. The browser does not understand them.
blocks.3.items.0: <header> — the top part. Logo, site title, usually the menu too.
blocks.3.items.2: <main> — the main content. Exactly one per page.
blocks.3.items.3: <article> — a standalone thing. An article, a listing, a comment.
blocks.3.items.4: <section> — a section of something. Part of a whole.
blocks.3.items.5: <footer> — the bottom part. Copyright, contacts, the fine print.
blocks.4.text: There is also <aside> — something off to the side that is not part of the main thing: an advert, similar listings, a sidebar.
blocks.6.text: Everyone gets confused here, so here is the test: lift the thing out of the page and put it somewhere else. Does it make sense on its own?
blocks.7.text: A bike listing — yes. An article — yes. A comment — yes. Those are <article>.
blocks.8.text: An About us section on its own? It means nothing. That is a <section>.
blocks.10.text: <main> is special: only one on the entire page. It says: this is the point, the rest is setting.
blocks.11.text: The menu is not in it. The footer is not in it. The logo is not in it. They repeat on every page — they are not the point of THIS one.
blocks.12.text: Notice what you achieve: the screen reader collects these tags into a list of landmarks. A blind person presses one key and jumps straight to the main content — skipping the menu they have already heard fifty times. You wrote no code for that. You just told the truth in your markup.
blocks.14.text: Run it before and after. No difference. None at all.
blocks.15.text: This is the third thing in this course that is invisible and matters. The first was <title>. The second was alt. Now this.
blocks.16.text: You are starting to see the pattern: HTML is full of things that are not for the eye.
blocks.17.text: I do not write HTML for the browser. I write it for the ones who are not looking at the screen.
blocks.18.text: And the most practical part: these tags cannot be added later. CSS changes in an hour. A style gets redone. But if you wrote the page out of groups, the meaning is not lost — it was never there.
blocks.19.text: Fix the code on the right.
blocks.20.text: And note: this is not a task about looks. You are rewriting the page for people who will never see it.
blocks.22.text: There is a test that takes thirty seconds and needs nothing at all.
blocks.23.text: Close your eyes. Read your code aloud — only the tags, none of the content.
blocks.24.text: Header. Nav. List of two links. Main. Article. Heading two. Paragraph. Footer.
blocks.25.text: Do you hear the structure of a page? If yes — you did it.
blocks.26.text: Now try it with the old code: Div. Div. List. Div. Div. Heading. Div.
blocks.28.text: That is exactly what someone who cannot see your screen hears. It is not an exercise — it is their daily life.
blocks.29.text: And the best part: this test needs no tools. Only ears.
